name: Fort Duquesne Bridge
funFact: This massive bridge carries six lanes of highway traffic and connects downtown Pittsburgh to the North Shore — home of PNC Park and Acrisure Stadium. It's one of the longest cantilever truss bridges in Pennsylvania!

name: Roberto Clemente Bridge
funFact: On Pittsburgh Pirates home game days, this bridge closes to cars so fans can walk across! It's named for Pirates legend Roberto Clemente, who was famous for both his amazing baseball skills and his kind heart helping others.

name: Andy Warhol Bridge
funFact: Named for Pittsburgh-born pop artist Andy Warhol! In 2009, a group of knitters covered this entire bridge in colorful yarn — a craft called 'yarn bombing' — to celebrate its 83rd birthday. It took 10,000 hours!

name: Rachel Carson Bridge
funFact: This bridge and its two sisters (the Clemente and Warhol bridges) are the only set of three identical self-anchored suspension bridges in the entire world! It's named for Rachel Carson, a western Pennsylvania scientist who helped start the modern environmental movement.

name: David McCullough Bridge
funFact: Named for historian David McCullough, who grew up in Point Breeze, Pittsburgh. Look closely at the ornate towers — they're decorated with beautiful sculpted eagles, shields, and intricate metalwork that took skilled craftsmen years to create!

name: 31st Street Bridge
funFact: This classic truss bridge connects the Strip District to the Deutschtown neighborhood on the North Side, one of Pittsburgh's oldest neighborhoods settled by German immigrants in the 1800s.

name: 40th Street Bridge
funFact: Built during the Roaring Twenties, this bridge connects the hip Lawrenceville neighborhood to the Borough of Millvale. From the middle of the bridge you can see Pittsburgh's hillside neighborhoods climbing steeply away from the river in both directions!

name: 62nd Street Bridge
funFact: This bridge has been connecting Morningside on the Pittsburgh side to Millvale on the north bank since 1927. Millvale is famous for its incredible murals painted inside Saint Nicholas Croatian Catholic Church by artist Maxo Vanka.

name: Highland Park Bridge
funFact: This arch bridge is right next to the Pittsburgh Zoo and PPG Aquarium! The zoo opened in 1898 and today is home to over 4,000 animals. Cross this bridge and you might hear the lions roar!

name: Washington Crossing Bridge
funFact: This bridge is named in honor of George Washington, who crossed the Allegheny River near this area in 1753 on a dangerous winter mission during the French and Indian War — when he was just 21 years old!

name: Hulton Bridge
funFact: Just a mile from this bridge sits the famous Oakmont Country Club, which has hosted more major golf championships than any other course in America! Presidents, celebrities, and the world's best golfers have played there.

name: Fort Pitt Bridge
funFact: Drivers coming out of the Fort Pitt Tunnel get Pittsburgh's most famous surprise — the entire downtown skyline suddenly appears! This magical 'Pittsburgh revealed' moment is considered one of the best highway entrances to any city in the world.

name: Smithfield Street Bridge
funFact: Built in 1883, this is Pittsburgh's oldest bridge still open to traffic! Its lenticular (lens-shaped) truss design was an engineering marvel of its time. The ornate lamp posts and metalwork make it one of the most beautiful bridges in the city.

name: Liberty Bridge
funFact: One of the busiest bridges in all of Pittsburgh — tens of thousands of cars cross Liberty Bridge every single day! It carries people between downtown and the South Hills neighborhoods, and runs right next to the Liberty Tubes tunnels.

name: Birmingham Bridge
funFact: Pittsburgh's only cable-stayed bridge over a major river! You can recognize it instantly by its tall towers with cables fanning out like a giant harp. It was named for the old Birmingham neighborhood, now part of the South Side.

name: Hot Metal Bridge
funFact: This bridge gets its awesome name from its original job — it carried ladles of liquid hot molten steel across the river between steel mills! Now it's a peaceful trail for walkers and bikers, part of the Great Allegheny Passage trail system.

name: Glenwood Bridge
funFact: This bridge carries I-837, which connects the Mon Valley communities to Pittsburgh. From its deck, you can see miles of the Monongahela River winding through the valley where Pittsburgh's legendary steel industry once roared day and night.

name: Homestead Grays Bridge
carries: US-30 / Homestead High Level Bridge Road
funFact: Named for the Homestead Grays, one of the greatest teams in Negro League baseball history! Slugger Josh Gibson — sometimes called the 'Black Babe Ruth' — was their star player. Below this bridge once stood the massive Homestead Steel Works, one of the world's most famous steel mills.

name: Rankin Bridge
funFact: This bridge carries the historic Lincoln Highway (US-30), one of the very first coast-to-coast roads in America! Built in 1913, the Lincoln Highway stretched from New York City all the way to San Francisco — a 3,400-mile adventure!

name: McKeesport–Duquesne Bridge
funFact: McKeesport was once called 'Tube City' because it was the center of America's steel pipe and tube manufacturing — the pipes that carry water and gas to homes across the country were made right here in this Mon Valley town!

name: West End Bridge
funFact: Completed in 1932, the same year as the famous Sydney Harbour Bridge in Australia! At night, the West End Bridge is beautifully illuminated and reflects in the Ohio River. It marks the spot where Pittsburgh's three rivers meet the Ohio and flow all the way to the Mississippi!

name: McKees Rocks Bridge
funFact: Right nearby is the McKees Rocks Mounds — a prehistoric burial site built by Native Americans over 2,000 years ago! The people who built these mounds are known as the Adena culture, and their earthworks are some of the oldest structures in western Pennsylvania.

name: Emsworth Bridge
funFact: Just downstream are the Emsworth Locks and Dam, which work like a giant elevator for boats — raising and lowering water levels so large river barges can travel safely along the Ohio River. Without it, the water would be too shallow for big boats!

name: Coraopolis Bridge
funFact: Coraopolis comes from the Greek word for 'maiden city.' Just a few miles from this bridge is Pittsburgh International Airport — so cross this bridge and you might look up and see planes coming in to land overhead!

name: Sewickley Bridge
funFact: The Borough of Sewickley is named after a Lenape (Delaware) word believed to mean 'sweet water.' It's one of Pittsburgh's most historic river towns, and the view of the Ohio River from this bridge is one of the prettiest in the entire region!

name: Fort Wayne Railroad Bridge
carries: Norfolk Southern railroad tracks and lower-deck pedestrian/bicycle trail
funFact: This bridge has an active upper railroad deck and a lower deck used by trail users to cross the Allegheny near Point State Park.
